coredata.dc:title: Barrier-breaking effects of ultrasonic cavitation for drug delivery and biomarker release

Wei, Jianpeng
Shen, Yuanyuan
Chen, Siping
Chen, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Recently, emerging evidence has demonstrated that cavitation actually creates important bidirectional channels on biological barriers for both intratumoral drug delivery and extratumoral biomarker release. To promote the barrier-breaking effects of cavitation for both therapy and diagnosis, we first reviewed recent technical advances of ultrasound and its contrast agents (microbubbles, nanodroplets, and gas-stabilizing nanoparticles) and then reported the newly-revealed cavitation physical details. In particular, we summarized five types of cellular responses of cavitation in breaking the plasma membrane (membrane retraction, sonoporation, endocytosis/exocytosis, blebbing and apoptosis) and compared the vascular cavitation effects of three different types of ultrasound contrast agents in breaking the blood-tumor barrier and tumor microenvironment. Moreover, we highlighted the current achievements of the barrier-breaking effects of cavitation in mediating drug delivery and biomarker release. We emphasized that the precise induction of a specific cavitation effect for barrier-breaking was still challenged by the complex combination of multiple acoustic and non-acoustic cavitation parameters. Therefore, we provided the cutting-edge in-situ cavitation imaging and feedback control methods and suggested the development of an international cavitation quantification standard for the clinical guidance of cavitation-mediated barrier-breaking effects.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Current achievements and remaining challenges of cavitation-mediated tumor therapy and diagnosis. (A) Graphical illustration highlighting the heterogeneity of tumors [2]. (B) Histological staining of a tumor xenograft illustrating that the therapeutic effects of gemcitabine was limited by the blood–tumor barrier [5]. (C) Graphical illustration summarizing biomedical application potential of cavitation-mediated barrier-breaking effects. TME: tumor microenvironment.

Ultrasound and its contrast agent-mediated cavitation events. (A) Spatial focusing ability of ultrasound. (B) Ultrasound focused through the human skull could break the BBB with high spatial resolution [24]. (C) Ultrasound broke the murine BBB with sub-millimeter spatial precision [25]. (D) Graphical illustrations of the characteristics of spontaneous bubbles and synthetic UCAs. (E) Difference in the tumor accumulation efficiency of non-targeting and targeting microbubbles conjugated with iRGD or/and CCR2 antibodies [10]. (F) and (G) are lipid shedding effects induced by microbubbles with spherical and non-spherical oscillations [43,44].

Cellular responses to cavitation-mediated barrier-breaking. (A) Graphical illustration demonstrating five different types of cellular cavitation responses. (B) and (C) present cavitation-induced retraction of cell membranes [67]. (D) Real-time confocal microscope recording reveals the reversible and irreversible sonoporation of cell membranes [70]. (E) Co-localization of FITC-dextran (green) and LysoTracker® (orange) demonstrating the endocytosis induced by cavitation [14]. (F) Co-localization of GFP (green) and LAMP-1 (red) demonstrating the exocytosis induced by cavitation [75]. (G) Membrane blebbing dynamics observed after cavitation [82]. (H) SEM images presenting the morphology of the intact and apoptotic cells treated with cavitation [86].

Multiple physical parameters involved in cavitation-mediated cellular barrier-breaking. (A), (B) and (C) summarize the acoustic, non-acoustic and cavitation parameters that determine the degree of cavitation-induced cellular damage, respectively. MB: microbubble.

Vascular cavitation effects for the barrier-breaking effects. (A) and (B) are graphical illustrations of the different cavitation effects induced by MBs and NDs, respectively, on blood vessels. (C) Cavitation and translation of MBs observed in smaller microvessels [106]. (D) Clustering and fusion of MBs observed in larger microvessels [108]. (E) MB cavitation disrupted vascular endothelial cells and their tight contact [68]. (F) Internalization of targeted MBs into endothelial cells [113]. (G) Leakage of dextran molecules from different types of blood vessels (Top, arterioles; middle, venules; bottom, capillaries) after cavitation [114]. (H) Comparison of the barrier-breaking efficiency of MBs and NDs [119]. (I) MBs produced by the phase shift of NDs in blood vessels (left) and tumor tissue (right) [17]. (J) Increased intratumoral delivery efficiency of doxorubicin mediated by NDs [50]. (K) Comparison of different contrast agents in breaking vascular barriers. MB: microbubble. ND: nanodroplet. US: ultrasound. SV: SonoVue microbubble.

Cavitation imaging and feedback control. (A) Summarization of the physical parameters of ultrasound and UCA used for cavitation-mediated drug delivery and biomarker release. (B) Cavitation dose calculation algorithm for active and passive cavitation imaging. (C) 3D volumetric cavitation image (color-coded) merged with B-mode ultrasound image (gray-scale) [16]. (D) Extravascular cavitation image (magenta color) merged with fluorescence image of nanocups (green color) [120].

row.entry: Freq.: 250 kHz; PNP: 0.1–0.5 MPa; PD: 10–20%; Time: 30–60 s.
A three-dimensional acoustic field-based conformal transfection (AFCT) system increased in-vitro transfection efficiency to ∼ 30%.
Murine breast tumor model (HCC827 cells and NDL cells)

Microbubble: Targeting microbubbles (VisualSonicsTM) loaded with CD326 antibody, 0.75 μm
Conc.: 2 × 107/mL
Freq.: 250 kHz; PNP: 100–500 kPa; PD: 4 ms; PRF: 30 Hz; Time: 3 min.

The maximum in-vitro transfection efficiency (∼33%) was achieved at a PNP of 500 kPa. In-vivo transfection increased IFN-β secretion from 1.1 pg/mg to 3.8 pg/mg and enhanced T cell recruitment (3.4-fold).

Microbubble: Dual-targeting microbubbles loaded with iRGD-peptide and CCR2 antibodies, 1.32 ± 0.22 μm
Conc.: 1 × 105/mL
Freq.: 1 MHz; PRF: 1 kHz; PD: 30%; Intensity:1.65 W/cm2; Time: 30 s.
Targeted microbubble increased in-vitro transfection efficiency to ∼ 30%. In-vivo transfection decreased the expression of AKT2 protein and inhibited tumor growth.
Nrf2 protein was hardly detected 48 h after ultrasound and nanobubble-mediated delivery. The treatment also sensitized M14 cells to cisplatin treatment.
siRNA (SIK2 and anti-miR21) nanocomplex and paclitaxel
Freq.: 1 MHz; PD: 50%; Intensity:1.8 W/cm2; Time: 10 s
Ultrasound and microbubble (USMB) resulted in a 2.5-fold increase in RNA delivery in EOC xenografts and USMB + siSIK2 + paclitaxel combination treatment reduced tumor volumes by ∼93%.
B16F10 cells and SCC-7 tumor model

Microbubble: 1.72 ± 0.44 μm
Concentration data not given
Freq.: 1.5 MHz; PD: 5%; PRF: 1 Hz; ISPTP: 10 W/ cm2; Time: 30 s.
Both penetration and accumulation of siRNA-nanoparticle were enhanced in tumor tissues. The treatment achieved sufficient VEGF-A inhibition and significantly inhibited tumor growth.
Nanobubbles enhanced the delivered siRNA into cancer cells and significantly increased the apoptosis rate of cancer cells to 52.8%.

Microbubble: ∼ 1.3 μm
Concentration data not given
Freq.: 1.03 MHz; PD: 50%; Intensity:1 W/cm2; Time: 1–3 min
In-vitro transfection down-regulated nearly 80% of HIF 1α mRNA. Synergistic treatment using ultrasound and laser irradiation completely inhibited the tumor growth.
Microbubble: diameter and concentration data not given
Freq.: 1 MHz; PD: 20%; PRF: 1 Hz; Intensity: 0.6–1 W/cm2; Time: 60 s.
Ultrasound-targeted microbubble destruction caused higher drug accumulation (1.88–3.33 folds) in glioma and increased the median survival durations of tumor-bearing mice to 52 days.
Freq.: 1 MHz; PNP: 0.3 MPa; PD: 10 ms; PRF: 1 Hz; Time: 1 min.
Microbubbles showed high payload capacity (efficiency of 69.4 ± 9.1%, payload of 1.4 mg/mL) of doxorubicin and provided 3.7-fold suppression of tumor growth.
Freq.: 3.0 MHz; PD: 0.0087%; PRF: 10 Hz; Power: 60 %; MI: 0.97; Time: 10 min.
Ultrasound stimulation released doxorubicin loaded on the cross-linked microbubbles in a rate of 6-times higher and promoted doxorubicin deposition in tumors.
Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma model (SU-DIPG-17 cells)
Freq.: 1.78 MHz; PRF: 0.67 Hz; PD: 10 ms; Time: 3 min.
MRI-guided focused ultrasound enhanced doxorubicin delivery by 4-fold compared to controls and significantly suppressed the volumetric tumor growth rate (24.4 ± 10.9%).

Microbubble: diameter data not given
Conc.: 3 × 108/mL
Freq.: 1 MHz; PNP: 1 MPa; PD: 1%; PRF:0.5 Hz.
Ultrasound and microbubble-mediated gemcitabine delivery delayed the time for tripling tumor volume to 25.6 ± 6.8 days and reduced the acute intestinal toxicity of gemcitabine.
Freq.: 1.5 MHz; PNP: 0.7 MPa; PD: 1 ms; PRF:5Hz; Time: 120 s.
Ultrasound and microbubble increased etoposide concentration in tumor tissue by 8-fold and decreased tumor growth by 45% as compared to without ultrasound.
Murine Breast tumor model (4 T1 cells)

Nanodroplet: 266.2 nm
Concentration data not given
Freq.: 1 MHz; Intensity: 1.5 W/cm2; Time: 1 min.

Ultrasound-triggered release selectively and efficiently accumulated hydroxychloroquine-loaded nanodroplets in tumors. The treatment inhibited tumor growth with an inhibition value of 80.02% and inhibits tumor lung metastasis
Freq.: 1.05 MHz; PD: 23.8 ms; PRF: 0.5–1 Hz; Time: 50–270 s.
Patients with clear BBB disruption (n = 11) had a median overall survival (OS) of 8.64 months compared with that of 8.64 months with no or poor BBB disruption (n = 8).
Freq.: 1.5 MHz; PNP: 845 kPa; PD: 100 µs; PRF: 1 kHz; Time: 5 s.

Cavitation increased levels of CD9 and CD63 exposing-EVs as well as the total number of EVs nearly 2-fold, 4-fold and 2-fold, respectively. EVs released by cavitation had a heterogeneous size distribution profile (30–1200 nm).
Freq.: 1.5 MHz; PNP: 0.83–1.96 MPa; Time: 1–10 min.
Compared to the control group, hemoglobin (HGB) release increased discontinuously with the increase in acoustic pressure from 10.25% to 42.60%.
Freq.: 1 MHz; PD: 20%, 50%; Intensity:2, 4, 6 W/cm2; Time: 6 min.
The cavitation-enhanced release of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) from LS174T cells steadily increased with increasing ultrasound treatment time and intensity (3- to 4-fold) compared to controls.
Freq.: 500 kHz; PNP: 570 kPa; PD: 32 µs; PRF: 1 kHz; Time: 1.5, 11.5, 21.5 min.

Cavitation increased the release of Green fluorescent protein (GFP), transferrin (Tfn), and lysosomal associated membrane protein-1 (LAMP-1) proteins from viable cells by 19%, 23%, and 50%, respectively.
Freq.: 1 MHz; PNP: 0.22, 0.76, 1.7 MPa; PRF: 100 Hz; PD: 50%; Time: 1 min.
The release of Mammaglobin mRNA and miR-21 increased with increasing nanodrop concentration, with a 25-fold and 42-fold increase at 2% concentration compared to pre-ultrasound levels, respectively.
Freq.: 1 MHz; PD: 20%; Intensity: 2 W/cm2; Time: 30 s.
Cavitation efficiently released miRNA, miR-21, miR-30e, miR-192 and miR-194 (10.9-fold, 7.17-fold, 5.95-fold and 5.36-fold, respectively) from tumor cells while maintaining cellular health.
Freq.: 1.15 MHz; PNP: 10 MPa; PD: 1000 cycles; Time: 30 s
Cavitation significantly enhanced the release of EVs (∼100-fold) and miR21-5p (2.3-fold) from tumors into the blood without increasing the number of circulating tumor cells in blood.
Freq.: 0.65 MHz; PNP: 1.5 MPa; PRF: 1 Hz; PD: 10 ms; Time: 3 min

Cavitation increased glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) and myelin basic protein (MBP) from 0.156 ± 0.068 ng/mL and 0.091 ± 0.034 ng/mL in pre-FUS blood samples to 0.353 ± 0.149 ng/mL and 0.364 ± 0.159 ng/mL in post-FUS blood samples, respectively.
miRNA (miR-9, miR-196a, miR-34c, miR-129-5p, miR-100, miR-16)
Freq.: 1.5 MHz; PNP: 78–90 MPa; PNP: 16–17 MPa; PD: 1–10 ms; PRF: 1 Hz; Time: 30 s.
Cavitation increased plasma miRNA from 3-fold to 32-fold within 15 min after the initiation of pulsed focused ultrasound and the release increases persisted for 3 h.
Freq.: 1.44 MHz; PRF: 1 Hz; PD: 10 ms; PNP: 0.59 MPa, 1.29 MPa, 1.58 MPa
Cavitation-induced eGFP mRNA release from tumors into the blood circulation (55-fold, 2000-fold, and 8000-fold) increased with ultrasound pressure (0.59 MPa, 1.29 and 1.58 MPa), respectively.
Mouse and pig glioblastoma model (U87-EGFRvIII cells)
Freq.: 1.5 MHz; PNP: 1.0 MPa; PRF: 5 Hz; PD: 6.7 ms; Time: 3 min
In mice and pigs, cavitation significantly increased plasma levels of EGFRvIII ctDNA (920- and 270-fold, respectively) and TERT C228T ctDNA (10- and 9-fold, respectively).
Freq.: 1 MHz; PNP: 0.3–0.4 MPa; PRF: 1 Hz; PD: 25 ms; Time: 2 min

A noticeable increase in cfDNA emerged at 15-minute post-cavitation. Two sequential sonication significantly elevated cfDNA levels (64.32 ng/mL) compared to other treatment group (single sonication: 22.54 ng/mL, MB only: 7 ∼ 8 ng/mL).
Protein (Endothelin-1, CA125, CA15-3) and miRNA (miR-21, miR-363, miR-490)
Freq.: 1.1 MHz; Power: 100–200 W; Time: 20 s.

MRI-guided cavitaiton increased release of endothelin-1 (78.8%), CA125 (9.95%) and CA15-3 (2.54%) in all patients. MRg-FUS significantly enhanced the release of miR-21 (2.7-fold), miR-363 (5.4-fold) and miR-490 (5.04-fold) in 1 out of 5 patients.
Freq.: 220 kHz; Sonication volume: 7.8 ± 6.0 cm3; Time: 111 ± 39 min.
MRI-guided cavitaiton acutely enhanced the release of plasma cfDNA (2.6 ± 1.2-fold), neuron-derived extracellular vesicles (3.2 ± 1.9-fold) and brain-specific protein S100b (1.4 ± 0.2-fold).

head.ce:title: Barrier-breaking effects of ultrasonic cavitation for drug delivery and biomarker release

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, emerging evidence has demonstrated that cavitation actually creates important bidirectional channels on biological barriers for both intratumoral drug delivery and extratumoral biomarker release. To promote the barrier-breaking effects of cavitation for both therapy and diagnosis, we first reviewed recent technical advances of ultrasound and its contrast agents (microbubbles, nanodroplets, and gas-stabilizing nanoparticles) and then reported the newly-revealed cavitation physical details. In particular, we summarized five types of cellular responses of cavitation in breaking the plasma membrane (membrane retraction, sonoporation, endocytosis/exocytosis, blebbing and apoptosis) and compared the vascular cavitation effects of three different types of ultrasound contrast agents in breaking the blood-tumor barrier and tumor microenvironment. Moreover, we highlighted the current achievements of the barrier-breaking effects of cavitation in mediating drug delivery and biomarker release. We emphasized that the precise induction of a specific cavitation effect for barrier-breaking was still challenged by the complex combination of multiple acoustic and non-acoustic cavitation parameters. Therefore, we provided the cutting-edge in-situ cavitation imaging and feedback control methods and suggested the development of an international cavitation quantification standard for the clinical guidance of cavitation-mediated barrier-breaking effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last decade’s global fight against cancer, one important consensus that has been reached is that the highly heterogeneous nature of solid tumors challenges the outcomes of clinical cancer therapy [1]. As presented in Fig. 1 A, even for a single tumor, different microenvironment components (i.e., chronic inflammation, hypoxia, vasculature, rigid matrix) and different cell populations (i.e., genomic instability, cancer stem cells, epigenetics, invasion, and metastasis) exist [2,3]. Moreover, the distribution of blood vessels, which function as the blood–tumor barrier (BTB), is also heterogeneous in solid tumors. This heterogeneity of BTB making tumor regions distal to the vasculature drug-resistant. For example, Primeau et al. found that the concentration of the anti-tumor drug doxorubicin decreases exponentially with increasing distance from blood vessels, and the drug therefore failed to kill cancer cells located 90–140 µm from blood vessels [4]. Using gemcitabine, another anti-cancer drug, Minchinton and Tannock also confirmed that highly proliferate cells in S-phase (stained in black) persisted in these hypoxic tumor areas (stained in green) distal to blood vessels (stained in blue) after 48 h of drug treatment (Fig. 1B) [5]. Therefore, to achieve homogenous cancer therapy, it is of great importance to controllably break down biological barriers in the solid tumors so that therapeutics could be effectively delivered from the blood vessels and into different tumor microenvironments.

From the view of cancer diagnosis, biological barriers in solid tumors also need controllable breaking to release sufficient molecular biomarkers. It is known that the early diagnosis and treatment of tumors before the occurrence of metastasis can significantly improve survival outcomes [6]. As one cancer diagnosis method, liquid biopsy-based biomarker detection can help to determine the presence, subtype, stage, and potential therapeutic outcomes of cancer [7]. Here, biomarker refers to cell-freeDNA (cfDNA), circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), messenger RNA (mRNA), microRNA (miRNA), proteins/peptides, and small signaling molecules. Although it has been reported that biomarkers can be used to detect cancer with a sensitivity as high as 98% in patients with confirmed cancer, this method for detecting early cancer is challenged by the extremely low biomarker levels in tumors as well as normal human physiological noise [8]. Therefore, these intratumoral biological barriers require controllable breaking to release biomarkers for liquid biopsy, including the nucleus and plasma membranes enveloping cell-level biomarkers, and the tumor microenvironment and blood vessel endothelium separating tissue-level biomarkers.

Overcoming biological barriers in solid tumors can benefit both drug delivery and biomarker release for both cancer therapy and diagnosis. Ultrasound, alone or together with micrometer- and nanometer-sized ultrasound contrast agents (UCAs), can induce localized cavitation activities. Cavitation has been reported to mechanically break biological barriers with several advantages, the first of which is its non-invasive spatial targeting ability. Ultrasound transducers placed on the skin can non-invasively focus ultrasound energy deep (3–12 cm) and precisely (spatial resolution of 1–2 mm) into tumor regions to overcome barriers [9]. The second advantage is the molecular targeting and drug-loading abilities of UCAs. Targeting antibodies loaded on the shells can enable UCAs to recognize and reside in tumor regions [10,11]. In addition, therapeutic drugs can be loaded on the shells of UCAs to achieve local drug delivery [12,13]. The third advantage is controllable breaking efficiency. The dimensions and reversibility of bidirectional channels created on biological barriers via cavitation can be controlled by programmable ultrasound parameters [14]. The fourth advantage is the measurable breaking safety. Note that both the spatial distribution of UCAs and the intensity of cavitation-mediated barrier breakdown can be monitored in real-time using cavitation imaging [15,16].

As summarized in Fig. 1C, ultrasound-driven cavitation has been reported to successfully break blood–tumor barriers, the tumor microenvironment, and the plasma membrane of tumor cells [17,18]. Cancer therapies delivered into tumors by cavitation include plasmid DNA, small interfering RNA (siRNA), chemotherapeutic drugs, photosensitizers, and sonosensitizers. Biomarkers released from tumors by cavitation include small signal molecules, protein/peptide, mRNA/miRNA, cfDNA/ctDNA, and extracellular vesicles (EVs). In particular, the bidirectional channels created by cavitation on biological barriers also facilitate cancer immunotherapy in that the cavitation-released tumor-specific antigens and the cavitation-upregulated pro-inflammatory cytokines can activate anti-tumor immunity [19,20].

Although ultrasound-driven cavitation demonstrates great promise in cancer therapy and diagnosis, the cellular responses and vascular effects of cavitation-mediated barrier-breaking effects are still complex. Moreover, the technical cross-comparison of cavitation-mediated pre-clinical outcomes still remains difficult because different research teams have employed different ultrasound and UCA systems [21]. In this review, we comprehensively summarized five types of cellular responses of cavitation (membrane retraction, sonoporation, endocytosis/exocytosis, blebbing and apoptosis) and compared the mechanistic difference of ultrasound contrast agents (microbubbles, nanodroplets and gas-stabilizing nanoparticles) in breaking the blood-tumor barrier and the tumor microenvironment. We emphasized the critical cavitation parameters for controlling the in-situ barrier-breaking effects and highlighted the latest research achievements of cavitation-mediated drug delivery and biomarker release. After showing the cutting-edge techniques for monitoring cavitation, we suggest that the urgent need of developing an international in-situ cavitation quantification standard to promote the clinical translation of cavitation-mediated barrier breakdown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-invasively focused ultrasound with improved spatial precision
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 2 A, ultrasound energy can be non-invasively focused into the deep tumor regions from transducers placed on the skin [22]. The focusing ability of ultrasound systems is evaluated by the fullwidthathalfmaximum (FWHM) of acoustic pressure [9]. In a currently available ultrasound trans-skull therapeutic system (i.e., ExAblate Neuro), ultrasound with a relatively low frequency of 220 kHz is used with spatial targeting ability of millimeter accuracy (2-mm transverse and 7-mm axial FWHM) [23]. In the pre-clinical treatment of Alzheimer’s disease, ultrasound focused to this precision resulted in intracranial cavitation and blood–brain-barrier (BBB) breaking in a volume of 318–873 mm3 (Fig. 2B) [24]. Using ultrasound of a higher frequency of 1.6 MHz, Lea-Banks et al. achieved cavitation-mediated BBB breaking in mice with sub-millimeter spatial precision of a diameter of 0.4–1.5 mm (Fig. 2C) [25]. Recently, in the research area of super-resolution ultrasonic imaging, the dimension of the acoustic focus has been further refined into the range of subwavelength (0.50–0.73λ) by using phononic metamaterial lens or core–shell shaped lens [26,27]. Considering the complexity of trans-skull ultrasound focusing, technical challenges still remain to precisely induce intracranial cavitation with a spatial resolution of subwavelength. Ultrasound transducer designs with flexible 3D-printed acoustic lens or array holders promise to solve this problem and achieve personalized trans-skull ultrasound focusing [28-30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UCAs with different cavitation scales and technical challenges

ce:section.ce:para: Because of the large acoustic impedance difference between tissue and gas, gas-cored agents can serve as UCAs to evoke cavitation effects. As presented in Fig. 2D, when the negative ultrasound pressure is sufficiently strong (greater than4.0 MPa for water), the bonds between the liquids molecules will be disrupted and gas cavities will be created as spontaneous UCAs [31]. Therefore, the cavitation scale of spontaneous UCAs is determined by the energy focus of the ultrasound to be in the millimeter range. Using a high-speed camera, Connor et al. directly observed that large gas bubbleswith a diameter of 10–100 µm weregeneratedusing ultrasound with 28.6 MPa of pressure, and bubble clouds of several millimeters were further generated using higher-pressure ultrasound (38.4 MPa) [32]. Except for its relatively large cavitation scale, the high ultrasound pressure required to generate spontaneous gas nuclei also hinders the safety of this technique. To solve these problems, one promising technical advance has been made by using catheters (2.5–7.0 mm in diameter) to precisely deliver high-pressure acoustic waves and generate bubble clouds [33].

Synthetic UCAs can induce micrometer- to millimeter-scale cavitation with ultrasound energy and UCA concentration as determinant parameters. Synthetic microbubbles with fluorinated gas cores (C3F8/C4F10) and various shell materials were produced with a uniform size distribution (1–5 µm) (Fig. 2D, the left image) [34,35]. The shell materials of synthetic microbubbles include denatured proteins (15–20 nm thick), polymers/copolymer (100–200 nm thick), and phosphatidylcholine lipids (approximately 3 nm thick) [36]. Functional materials have been added to the microbubble shells, such as cationic lipid for gene-loading microbubbles, liposomes for drug-loading microbubbles, and antibodies for tumor-targeting microbubbles [35]. Different from the passive targeting ability of ultrasound focus, the active targeting ability of antibodies conjugated on the shells can efficiently accumulate microbubbles in tumors. Liu et al. conjugated iRGD or/and CCR2 antibodies on the microbubble shells and therefore increased the tumor-targeting and tumor-accumulating ability of microbubbles (indicated by green fluorescence) (Fig. 2E) [10]. However, the application of microbubbles in some sophisticated therapy and diagnosis is still challenged by their short circulation times (half-life of 3–15 min) [37-40]. To improve the in vivo persistence of microbubbles, Chen et al. incorporated poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) polymer into the lipid shell of microbubbles [41]. Tsirkin et al. further showed that the addition of PLGA produced hard shell microbubbles with high stability in circulation and an extended cavitation time (approximately 2-fold) [42].

Another important synthetic UCA is the nanodroplet (diameter of 100–600 nm), which uses similar shell materials as microbubbles but has liquid cores of low boiling-point (BP) perfluoropentane (C5F12, BP = 29 °C) or perfluorohexane (C6F14, BP = 56 °C) [45,46]. When excited with ultrasound of high pressure (greater than2 MPa), liquid cores of the nanodroplets underwent phase shifts and vaporized to form microbubbles in the rarefaction cycle of ultrasound (Fig. 2D, the middle image) [45]. Compared with microbubbles, nanodroplets with sizes of several hundred nanometers are more advantageous for deeper penetration through blood vessel gaps and into tumor tissues [47]. Also, nanodroplets persisted for a longer time at 37 °C (half-life of 2–6 h) than microbubbles [48,49]. However, the pre-clinical translation of nanodroplets is still challenged by the high ultrasound pressure required for vaporization. To vaporize nanodroplets using safer ultrasound exposure, synergistic vaporization technique can be developed in the future. For example, using optical and acoustic energy for synergistic vaporization, Hu et al. showed that the ultrasound pressure threshold for efficiently vaporizing nanodroplets of 254.8 nm in diameter could be decreased to 0.82 MPa [50].

The newly-reported synthetic UCAs are the gas-stabilizing nanoparticles (i.e., nanocups, nanocones, and mesoporous silica nanoparticles) [51-54]. These nanoparticles have structural cavities that are capable of trapping nanobubbles against gas dissolution (Fig. 2D, the right image). The nanobubbles grew and detached from the cavities during the rarefactional pressure phase of ultrasound wave. Since the frequency of the driving ultrasound (0.5–2 MHz) was far below the resonance frequency of the nanobubbles (e.g., 120 MHz for 100-nm diameter), they mainly induced inertial cavitation and their inertial cavitation thresholds (e.g., 2 MPa for 100-nm diameter) decreased with increasing nanobubble diameters [51]. The cavitation activity mediated by gas-stabilizing nanoparticles sustained for at least four times longer than microbubbles [52]. However, the synthetic process of these nanoparticles were much more complicated than microbubbles.

Cavitation refers to the phenomenon that UCAs will periodically vibrate or violently collapse under ultrasound excitation. Using ultrasound parameter mechanical index (MI), cavitation was classified as either stable cavitation with microbubble vibration or inertial cavitation with microbubble collapse [55,56]. The exact type of cavitation was also determined by the pressure, the viscosity and the inertia of the surrounding medium as well as the shell materials and the surface tension of the microbubbles [57,58]. Both numerical modeling and cavitation imaging suggest that the stable cavitation exert mechanical effects via microstreaming whereas the inertial cavitation via microjetting. Cavitation microstreaming drives its surrounding fluid into a vortex motion (cross-form or sunflower-form) [59,60]. The reported velocity of fluid microstreaming varied largely from 6 mm/s to 525 cm/s, as determined by the ultrasound parameters, the boundary condition of the microbubble, and the viscosity of the liquid [59-61]. In cavitation microjetting, microbubbles experience rapid collapse and generate high-speed fluid jets and shock waves. The shock wave generated by cavitation jetting had a velocity of 5.5–157.74 m/s and an intensity as high as 1.8 GPa [62,63].

Recently, the release kinetics of microbubble payloads during cavitation have been revealed by using ultra-high-speed camera and high-speed fluorescence imaging. Luan et al. showed that, when a relative bubble oscillation amplitude exceeded 30%, lipid shedding from microbubble shells started to occur (Fig. 2F) [43]. As presented in Fig. 2G, Lajoinie et al. further revealed that non-spherical bubble oscillations first lead to local lipid oversaturation and then released the lipids at a velocity typically of the order of 0.1 m/s [44]. Note that the shedding effect of cavitation could directly deliver microbubble payloads into the cells or tumor spheroids in a manner called as sonoprinting [64,65]. More specifically, in sonoprinting, the nanomedicines were deposited into the targets in the form of patches instead of concentration gradient-driven diffusion. It can be inferred that the cell or tumor barriers were wounded during sonoprinting. However, the question whether biomarkers were also rapidly released right during the microbubble cavitation has not been answered.

As presented in Fig. 2A, we summarized the reported cellular responses associated with cavitation-mediated barrier-breaking effects according to their timescales as follows: membrane retraction (microseconds), sonoporation (seconds to minutes), endocytosis/exocytosis (minutes), membrane blebbing (minutes) and apoptosis (minutes to hours).

Marmottant and Hilgenfeldt first observed lipid bilayer membrane retraction induced by cavitation microstreaming using microscopic imaging of 10-ms interframe time [66]. Recently, using a ultra-high-speed camera and position-locating microbeads, Yuan et al. showed that cavitation jetting resulted in heterogeneous membrane retraction within 12 µs after cavitation (Fig. 3 B) and the maximum deformation strain decreased exponentially with cell-bubble distance [67]. They also revealed that, after cavitation, both the membrane and actin cytoskeleton contracted over 120 s even though the cell was intact (Fig. 3C). It is important to note that cavitation-induced membrane retraction could contribute to drug delivery and biomarker release by enlarging endothelial cell–cell junctions [68].

Sonoporation refers to the phenomenon by which cavitation breaks the lipid bilayer continuity and create pores on the cell membrane. The created pores can directly mediated bi-directional transmembrane transport. Using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Yang et al. reported the relationship between ultrasound pressure and pore size, with ultrasound of 0.19 MPa creating pores of 1–94.3 nm in diameter and ultrasound of 0.48 MPa creating pores of 2.03–4.31 μm in diameter [69]. Using high-resolution confocal microscope, Hu et al. revealed that the micrometer-scale pores first underwent expansion and then started to repair themselves [70,71]. They also revealed that cell membrane pores with areas smaller than 30 μm2 can reseal within 1 min, whereas pores larger than 100 μm2 cannot reseal. Moreover, they showed that non-displacing microbubbles resulted in circular disruptions while displacing microbubbles created elongated larger disruptions (Fig. 3D). van Rooji et al further showed that, compared with non-displacing microbubble, displacing microbubbles enhanced reversible sonoporation and preserved cell viability [72]. These evidences emphasize that the displacement of cavitation microbubbles induced by ultrasound radiation force should also be considered in sonoporation especially when using long ultrasound pulse duration for excitation.

Endocytosis and exocytosis events featured with nanoscale membrane budding protect cells against mechanical tension loading [73]. These events have been reported in cavitation-induced cellular responses. Meijering et al. suggested that the intracellular delivery of macromolecules (155- and 500-kDa dextrans) was mediated by cavitation-induced endocytosis [74]. De Cock et al. reported that microbubble cavitation driven by low acoustic pressure mainly resulted in the cellular uptake of FITC-dextran (2 MDa) via endocytosis within 15 min after excitation (Fig. 3E) [14]. In addition to endocytosis, Hussein et al. noted that the cavitation-wounded cells secreted large amounts of lysosomes via exocytosis to repair their membranes (Fig. 3F) [75]. Yuana et al. reported that cavitation increased exocytosis by cancer cells and therefore increased the number of extracellular vehicles (EVs) carrying CD9 and CD63a (2- and 4-fold, respectively) [76]. Note that bi-directional cellular transport mediated by endocytosis or exocytosis observed in cavitation were not localized at the microbubble sites.

Recent evidences demonstrated that the microbubble-cell interaction not only induced membrane-level phenomena but also triggered the disassembly and reassembly of the cytoskeleton [77,78]. The degree of cavitation-induced cytoskeleton damage increased with increasing acoustic pressure and decreased with increasing bubble-cell distances [79]. When the resealed membrane detached from the damaged cytoskeleton, the membrane expanded and a spherical membrane bleb (diameter of 30–2000 nm) formed [80]. Leow et al. showed that the size of membrane bleb was correlated with the diameter of the microbubble [81]. Jia et al. found that the reversible blebs underwent four phases (nucleation, expansion, pausing and retraction) while the irreversible blebs resulted in cell death [82] (Fig. 3G). They also concluded that the lifetime of membrane blebs (about tens of seconds to minutes) positively correlated with the cavitation-induced cell damage degree.

It has been reported that irreversible cellular cavitation damages (e.g., the excessive influx of calcium, the induction of reactive oxygen species, the depolarization of mitochondrial membrane potential, and the release of cytochrome c) could drive the cells to death via apoptosis and subsequently break cellular contents into apoptotic blebs [83-85]. As presented in Fig. 3H, large membrane blebs associated with apoptosis emerged on the cells treated with cavitation (US + MB) [86]. Note that cavitation-induced apoptosis was a long-term cellular event. Zhong et al. found that the number of cell apoptotic bodies peaked 12 h after cavitation [87]. In the scenario of cavitation-mediated drug delivery, cell apoptosis increased linearly with the acoustic energy of ultrasound and enhanced the therapeutic efficiency of anti-cancer drugs [86,88,89]. In the scenario of cavitation-mediated biomarker release, cell apoptosis resulted in the breaking of DNA double-strand and the release of cytosolic constituents. For example, Ashush et al. observed that the internal DNA fragments of the cavitation-damaged cells were released in the form of apoptotic bodies [90].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic challenge: Transport on the level of nuclear membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As for model drugs of low molecular weight (e.g., propidium iodide of 668.4 Da and fluorescent dextrans < 70 kDa), it has been reported that they passively diffused into the cells through cavitation-wounded membrane regions [74,91]. However, time delays were observed in the fluorescence intensity changes of these model drugs in the cytoplasm and nucleus areas, indicating a secondary diffusion mechanism exists on the nuclear membrane. The permeability of the nuclear pore complex (NPC), which controls the direct transport between the cytoplasm and the nucleoplasm, is regulated by Ca2+ [92]. Note that both the intracellular Ca2+ concentration and its storage were significantly affected by cavitation [93]. Except for the NPC, nuclear membrane and its integrity in cavitation also need future investigation. Recent evidence shows that nuclear membrane rupture and repair occurred in sonoporation [94]. Therefore, it is of great importance to clarify the biophysical impact of cavitation on the cell nucleus. As for therapeutic genes of macromolecular weights, their transport on the level of nuclear membrane is also left unknown. In particular, these macromolecules are found to enter the cavitation-wounded cells in the form of clusters instead of diffusion [64,74,95]. Therefore, the nuclear transport of the gene clusters after cavitation needs more mechanistic exploration so that their transfection efficiency can be further increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Technical challenges: Complex mechanisms determined by multiple parameters

ce:section.ce:para: As the energy input driving cavitation dynamics, ultrasound and its acoustic parameters play important roles in determining the specific cellular responses of cavitation. The acoustic parameters of the pulsed ultrasound include frequency (Freq.), pulse duration (PD), pulse repetition frequency (PRF), peak negative pressure (PNP), intensity, energy, and exposure time. As shown in Fig. 4 A, it is widely accepted that greater ultrasound intensities with higher PD, PRF and PNP result in larger cellular damages, therefore varying cellular responses from membrane retraction to apoptosis [14,87,96]. Note that the cellular responses such as membrane retraction, endocytosis, reversible sonoporation and blebbing, which break biological barriers without affecting the cell viability, contribute to the drug delivery application of cavitation. Membrane retraction, exocytosis and these cellular responses (e.g., apoptosis, irreversible sonoporation and blebbing) that eventually lead to cell death and disassembly contribute to the biomarker release application of cavitation. The challenge is that the exact cellular responses of cavitation are determined by the complex combination of ultrasound parameters. For example, under the same ultrasound energy settings (1–100 J), Tran et al. varied different ultrasound PD (18 μs–36 ms) and PNP (0.5–2.5 MPa) [97]. They found that prolonging the PD could decrease the PNP threshold and therefore increased both the delivery efficiency and the cell viability. Since different combinations of ultrasound parameters were used by different research groups to study the cellular effects of cavitation, controversial findings have been reported. For example, most of the cavitation-mediated intracellular delivery processes are site-specific, spatially corresponding to the position of the microbubbles [18,64]. However, recent evidence also suggests that DNA plasmids entered the cells in a global internalization manner [95].

The specific cellular responses of cavitation is also determined by non-acoustic parameters (Fig. 4B). The first types of non-acoustic parameters are associated with microbubbles (Fig. 4B). The degree of cavitation-induced cellular damage was found to increase with the diameter and number of microbubbles and decrease with the microbubble-cell distance [79,98]. It was also shown that the cellular outcomes of cavitation was not affected by the position of the microbubbles on the cells [68,99]. The second types of non-acoustic parameters are associated with the cells. For example, increasing extracellular matrix rigidity, which plays important roles in regulating the cell mechanics, led to larger cellular damage after cavitation [100,101]. There was not a consensus on the optimal mitosis phase for cavitation-mediated intracellular delivery. Fan et al. showed that cells in S phase of the cell cycle had the greatest membrane permeability [102]. Pichardo et al. showed that the cells in the G2&M phase had significantly higher transfection rate [103]. It is widely accepted that the cellular effects of cavitation varied with the cell type [94,103,104]. It is also found that the intracellular delivery outcomes of cavitation was irrespective of the cell size [68].

Parameters describing the microbubble cavitation dynamics directly determined the degree of cellular damages (Fig. 4C). Beekers et al. observed that sonoporation occurred when the parameter of microbubble excursion amplitude (also known as maximum oscillation radius) exceeded 0.7 μm [68]. Helfield et al. showed that, for ultrasound waves of different frequencies, the thresholds of microbubble excursion amplitude for achieving successful intracellular delivery varied from 0.8 μm to 2 μm [91]. Furthermore, they showed that the microbubble oscillation-induced shear-stress threshold for disrupting cell membrane was on the order of kilopascals (e.g., 14.1 kPa for 1-MHz ultrasound of 8 cycles). The cellular outcomes of cavitation was found to be independent of non-linear microbubble behavior that was quantified by the subharmonic and second harmonic echo signal levels [68]. The type of cavitation affected the delivery results of vesicles loaded on the microbubbles in that stable cavitation enhanced cell permeability and meanwhile increased cell viability [105]. Here, we would like to emphasize the importance of microbubble displacement in cavitation. Although the displacing microbubbles with cavitation dynamics resulted in larger plasma membrane damages (e.g., long strip-shaped wounds), the cells repaired themselves with agreaterchance [71,72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbubble-enhanced cavitation for blood-tumor barrier breaking
Microbubble cavitation, translation, clustering and fusion
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 5 A, microbubbles with a diameter of 1–5 µm circulate inside blood vessels and its cavitation effects mainly exert on vessel walls. In small microvessels (diameter of 10–80 µm), Chen et al. showed that vessel walls underwent rapid expansion or contraction according to microbubble cavitation phases (maximum displacement: ∼10 µm) and microbubble translation occurred with a maximum distance of larger than 20 µm (Fig. 5C) [106,107]. In large microvessels (diameter: ∼150 µm), Juang et al. showed that ultrasound radiation force resulted in greater microbubble translation and the microbubbles clustered near the vessel wall (indicated by red arrows, Fig. 5D) [108]. After cavitation, microbubbles fused with each other to form large bubbles. Note that the formation of microbubble clusters (also known as microbubble clouds) greatly amplified the local cavitation intensity in random sites of the blood vessels, therefore challenging the safety of cavitation-mediated blood-tumor barrier breaking [109]. Therefore, Morse et al. suggest that microbubble cavitation driven by ultrasound of rapid short pulses with reduced radiation force could break blood vessels in a more diffused and safer profile [110]. In addition, it should be noted that microbubble concentration also affect the degree of microbubble clustering in large blood vessels [111].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Endothelial cell responses mediating the intercellular and transcellular breaking
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely accepted the breaking of endothelial cell–cell junctions only occurred during microbubble cavitation [99,112]. However, recent evidences revealed that the secondary cellular responses induced by cavitation also contributed to the opening of endothelial monolayer. Beekers et al. showed that the cavitation-wounded endothelial cell constantly retracted for 202 s post-cavitation and intracellular gaps (indicated by white arrows, Fig. 5E) formed between the wounded cell and its neighboring cells [68]. Helfield et al. further showed that these intracellular gaps required more than 30–60 min to recover [91]. In addition to membrane retraction, Helfield et al. showed that the motility of the wounded endothelial cells increased for 4 h post-cavitation and the prolonged cell movement resulted in dynamic intercellular gap formation [18]. As for the cavitation-mediated transcellular breaking, it is newly revealed that endothelial cells could respond to the targeted microbubbles (shells conjugated with αvβ3/CD31 antibodies) and could internalize them. Therefore, the targeted microbubble created pores and tunnels in the endothelial monolayer more efficiently than the non-targeted microbubbles (Fig. 5F) [113].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spatial-temporal characteristics of blood-tumor barrier breaking
ce:section.ce:para: Using two-photon fluorescence microscopy, Poon et al. imaged the in-vivo process by which cavitation broke all types of blood vessels in the rat brain (Fig. 5G) [114]. However, their results also showed that the cavitation-mediated breaking was not achieved in all segments of the blood vessels, including 20% of total arteriole segments, 11% of total venule segments, and 5% of total capillary segments. To effectively and safely increase the degree of cavitation-induced blood-tumor barrier breaking, both the acoustic parameters (ultrasound pressure, PD, PRF, and exposure time) and the UCA parameters (microbubble diameter and concentration) should be carefully optimized [115-117]. Poon et al. also identified two types of cavitation-induced vascular leakage according to their plateau time: the fast one (<10 min) and the slow one (greater than10 min and < 4 h) [114]. In mice, Zhang et al. demonstrated that cavitation-induced BBB opening was reversible, recovering within 2 h post-cavitation [118]. In the first-in-human trial, Abrahao et al. observed the recovery of cavitation-wounded BBB 24 h after ultrasound excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanodroplet-enhanced cavitation for intratumoral barrier breaking
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasound radiation force exerted on the nanodroplets is negligible when compared with that of the microbubbles. Therefore, no obvious translation, clustering or fusion dynamics was found in the nanodroplets exposed to ultrasound excitation. This feature of the nanodroplets enabled them to enhance cavitation and break blood vessels in a more homogeneous manner. Using a nitrocellulose membrane to mimic the multi-layered pore structure of the BBB, Song et al. demonstrated that nanodroplets enabled more effective and homogenous trans-membrane diffusion of a 150-kDa tracer than microbubbles of the same concentration (∼5 × 108/mL) (Fig. 5H) [119]. Moreover, nanodroplets also differ from microbubbles in that, due to their nanometer-scale sizes, they can extravasate from the blood vessels and penetrate deep into tumor tissues (Fig. 5B). As presented in Fig. 5I, using high-pressure (10 MPa) ultrasound to vaporize the nanodroplets, microbubbles produced by the phase shift of nanodroplets (diameter: 359 ± 107 nm) were found in both blood vessels (left images) and tissues (right images) [17]. Hu et al. compared the breaking effects of nanodroplets delivered intravenously and intratumorally [50]. As illustrated in Fig. 5J, nanodroplets delivered intravenously mainly broke blood-tumor barriers and the distribution of the delivered drug (doxorubicin) was limited to the regions around the blood vessels. By contrast, nanodroplets delivered intratumorally destroyed the tumor microenvironment, and doxorubicin was diffused homogeneously inside the tumor tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocup-enhanced cavitation for intratumoral barrier breaking
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the acoustic property of the nanodroplets, nanocups that hold nanobubbles in their structural cavities can extravasate blood vessels and retain their ultrasound-responsive capabilities for initiating inter-tissue cavitation [120]. Furthermore, Mannaris et al. directly compared the difference of nanocups, nanodroplets and microbubbles in their barrier breaking efficiencies (Fig. 5K) [121]. The results showed that, for the lowest input ultrasound energy, nanocups enabled the most extensive model-drug extravasation from vessel-mimicking phantoms (spatial range of several millimeters). The results also demonstrated that longer ultrasound PD and exposure time led to greater extravasation of nanocups whereas higher ultrasound PRF resulted in less extravasation.

The tumor-targeting ability of ultrasound and UCAs as well as the drug-loading ability of UCAs enabled the barrier-breaking effects of cavitation to achieve local drug delivery in tumor regions with reduced non-specific biodistribution and systemic toxicity. As summarized in Table 1 , the therapeutics delivered into tumors via cavitation have included anti-cancer genes, chemotherapeutic drugs, photosensitizers, and sonosensitizers. Cavitation-mediated in vitro gene transfection efficiency with 3D acoustic field optimization or a targeted microbubble design was reported to have a maximum value of approximately 30% [10,11,122]. Using plasmid DNA encoding IFN-β for in vivo transfection, cavitation increased the level of IFN-β secretion from 1.1 to 3.8 pg/mg and enhanced T cell recruitment by 3.4-fold [11]. For siRNA and shRNA, which function to silence cancer-related mRNA, cavitation-medicated in vivo transfection significantly decreased the expression of the targeted proteins (AKT2 and Nrf2) in tumors [10,123].

Furthermore, in the combined chemo-gene therapy using siRNA and paclitaxel, cavitation enhanced both the penetration and accumulation of siRNA (SIK2, anti-miR21, VEGF, and Survivin) in tumor regions, resulting in efficient tumor growth inhibition [124-126]. In synergistic therapy using siRNA (HIF1α) and a photosensitizer (porphyrin), cavitation-mediated transfection down regulated nearly 80% of HIF1α mRNA, and the treatment completely inhibited tumor growth [127]. In addition, in synergistic therapy using a sonosensitizer (chlorin e6) and chemotherapeutic drug (hydroxychloroquine), cavitation resulted in higher drug accumulation (1.88–3.33-fold) in glioma and increased the median survival duration of tumor-bearing mice to 52 days [128].

For chemotherapy alone, cavitation selectively and efficiently enhanced the accumulation (4–8-folds) of drugs (paclitaxel, doxorubicin, gemcitabine, etoposide, and hydroxychloroquine) in tumor regions to improve the therapeutic efficiency [12,13,129-132]. Idbaih et al. reported a first-in-man clinical trial in patients with recurrent glioblastoma of the cavitation-enhanced trans-BBB delivery of a chemotherapeutic drug (carboplatin). In their work, patients with clear BBB disruption (n = 11) had median overall survival of 12.94 months, compared with 8.64 months in patients with no or poor BBB disruption (n = 8) [133].

Ultrasound-driven cavitation can be non-invasively induced in tumor regions to break biological barriers for amplifying and locating blood biomarkers [134]. As presented in Table 2 , researchers have conducted cellular, animal, and pre-clinical experiments to reveal the biomarker release details of cavitation. Cellular release studies confirmed that ultrasound-induced cavitation can efficiently enhance the release of hemoglobin (10.25%–42.60%), carcinoembryonic antigen (3–4-fold), GFP (19%), transferrin (23%), and lysosomal-associated membrane protein-1 (50%), and ultrasound pressure, intensity, and exposure time were the key release parameters [75,134,135]. In addition to protein biomarkers, Paproski et al. illustrated that cavitation enhanced the release of mammaglobin mRNA (25-fold) [136] and Teenan et al. found that cavitation increased the release of miR-21 (10.9-fold), miR-30e (7.17-fold), miR-192 (5.95-fold), and miR-194 (5.36-fold) [137].

In vivo release experiments demonstrated that cavitation increased tumor-derived glial fibrillary acidic protein (2.26-fold) and myelin basic protein levels (4-fold) in blood samples [138]. Chevillet et al. found that cavitation increased plasma miRNA levels by 3–32-fold [139], and Zhu et al. observed that the ultrasound pressure (0.59, 1.29, and 1.58 MPa) was correlated with the release efficiency of eGFP mRNA (55-, 2000-, and 8000-fold, respectively) [140]. In mice and pigs, Pacia et al. noted that cavitation significantly increased the plasma levels of EGFRvIII ctDNA (920- and 270-fold) and TERT C228T ctDNA (10- and 9-fold) [141]. Zhang et al. reported that cavitation increased cfDNA levels (8-fold) in blood circulation [142]. It was also identified that the optimal liquid biopsy time for cavitation-released biomarkers is 15 min after cavitation [139,142]. In patients with uterine fibroids, D’Souza et al. confirmed that cavitation increased the tumor release of endothelin-1 (78.8%), CA125 (9.95%), and CA15-3 (2.54%), although cavitation only enhanced the release of miR-21 (2.7-fold), miR-363 (5.4-fold), and miR-490 (5.04-fold) in one of five patients. In patients with glioblastoma, Meng et al. confirmed that MRI-guided cavitation enhanced the release of plasma cfDNA (2.6 ± 1.2-fold), the brain-specific protein S100b (1.4 ± 0.2-fold), and neuron-derived EVs (3.2 ± 1.9-fold). The cavitation-enhanced release of extracellular vesicles was also confirmed in cellular and animal studies (2- and 100-fold, respectively) [76,143]. As an emerging technique named sonobiopsy, both the feasibility and efficiency of ultrasound-driven cavitation for liquid biopsy have been proven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ cavitation imaging and feedback control

ce:section.ce:para: As summarized in Fig. 6 A, the efficiency of cavitation-mediated drug delivery and biomarker release was mainly controlled by ultrasound parameters (frequency, PNP, PD, PRF and exposure time) and UCA parameters (diameter, targeting ability and concentration). We also found that the reported ultrasound and UCA parameters (listed in table 1 and 2) for inducing therapeutic and diagnostic cavitation varied largely. Considering that researchers from different laboratories have been using different combinations of ultrasound and UCA parameters to induce cavitation, it is of great importance to create an international standard to quantitatively evaluate the in-situ dimensions and dose of cavitation.

Fig. 6B summarizes the cavitation imaging algorithms developed for in-situ calculation of cavitation dose. In active cavitation imaging, the imaging ultrasound transducers both sends pulses and receives echoes to quantify cavitation activity. Two algorithms, including singular value decomposition (lowest singular value for cavitation) and equivalent time relation (shifted frequency in slow-time spectrum for stable cavitation), have been recently used to distinguish cavitation regions from the tissue or water background [15,146]. In passive cavitation imaging, where the monitoring ultrasound transducers only receives echoes to quantify cavitation activity, the energy spectrum density (ESD) of harmonic emissions was calculated for the stable cavitation dose (SCD), and the ESD of wideband emission was calculated for the inertial cavitation dose [115]. Instead of frequency-domain filtering algorithms, time-domain beamforming algorithms have also been developed to calculate passive cavitation doses, such as time exposure acoustics (TEA), robust capon beamforming (RCB), eigenspace-based robust Capon beamforming (EISRCB), and robust linear programming beamforming (RLPB) [120,147].

Clinical translation of cavitation imaging algorithms for monitoring in-situ barrier-breaking effects is still challenged by: (i) planar ultrasound imaging systems cannot comprehensively quantify the 3D cavitation activity (e.g., the cavitation clouds); and (ii) localization errors of cavitation sites in passive cavitation images still exist. Recently, using a 2D matrix imaging array, Escudero et al. developed volumetric cavitation imaging for 3D cavitation monitoring (Fig. 6D) [16]. Moreover, Paverd et al. improved the location precision of passive cavitation imaging to 0.4 ± 0.2 mm using orthogonally-oriented imaging arrays (Fig. 6E) [120]. Using the PAX method, Gray et al. further reduced the co-localization error between passive cavitation imaging and B-mode imaging to the order of 0.1 mm [148].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Closed-loop feedback control of cavitation dose
ce:section.ce:para: To control the cavitation dose and consequently guarantee the safety and efficiency of drug delivery as well as biomarker release, real-time closed-loop feedback control between the calculated cavitation dose and ultrasound parameters (PNP and PRF) was established (Fig. 6). Different research groups controlled different ultrasound parameters to achieve the pre-set cavitation thresholds. For example, Cheng et al. and O’Reilly et al. adjusted PNP in their feedback systems using acoustic pressure steps of 0.01–0.03 MPa and 3 kPa, respectively [149,150]. In contrast, Maeda et al. monitored the cavitation dose and adjusted PRF with a feedback rate of 5 Hz to control the cavitation activity [151]. Cheng et al. further showed that the cavitation dose of flowing microbubbles was actually controlled by complex combinations of ultrasound PNP, PD and PRF [152].

Note that the cavitation thresholds set for feedback control in those studies were relative or normalized values that varied largely among different ultrasound systems. Wu et al. pointed out that the current spectrum-based methods for cavitation threshold calculation is controversial and they recommended a total integration of real cavitation noise over the full spectrum [153]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop an international cavitation quantification standard for cross-comparisons of cavitation thresholds and pre-clinical outcomes reported by different research groups.

Although the feasibility of ultrasound-driven cavitation for breaking biological barriers has been proven in both intratumoral drug delivery and extratumoral biomarker release, the precise induction of safe and efficient intracorporeal cavitation is still challenged by the complex combination of multiple acoustic and non-acoustic parameters affecting the type and intensity of in-situ cavitation. When applying cavitation for increasing plasma membrane permeability, we showed that there exist five types of cellular cavitation responses (membrane retraction, sonoporation, endocytosis/exocytosis, blebbing and apoptosis). When applying cavitation for breaking the blood-tumor barrier and the tumor microenvironment, we showed that three types of ultrasound contrast agents mediate different vascular cavitation effects. To monitor and control in-situ cavitation, we provided the cutting-edge cavitation imaging methods and suggested the urgent development of an international cavitation quantification standard for the clinical guidance of cavitation-mediated barrier-breaking effects.
